selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

response_raw:
{
    "mode": "human",
    "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
    "opinion": "As someone who doesn't use social media myself, I've watched from the sidelines as my children and grandchildren navigate this tricky world of online information. From my chair at the salon, I hear clients talk about things they've seen online that just don't sit right with me. Managing fake content starts with good old-fashioned common sense, the kind my parents taught me. When I hear something that sounds too good to be true or makes me feel anxious, I pause and think it through. I remember my mother saying 'consider the source' - that applies just as much to online posts as it did to neighborhood gossip. Since I'm naturally curious but also get worried easily, I've learned to channel that curiosity into checking facts rather than letting anxiety take over. I ask my husband or one of my kids to help me look things up on their computers when I hear something questionable. At work, when clients share wild stories they've seen online, I gently suggest they check with reliable sources like our local newspaper or news stations. My Christian faith teaches me about truth and honesty, and I think those values guide how I approach information - if something feels dishonest or manipulative, it probably is. I've taught my children to be skeptical of anything that tries to play on their emotions too strongly, especially since I know how easily anxiety can cloud judgment. The board games we play as a family have taught us all to think several moves ahead, to consider consequences - that same thinking helps when evaluating online content. My painting hobby has shown me how perspective matters; what looks one way from one angle might be completely different from another. Really, dealing with fake content comes down to trusting your gut, asking questions, and remembering that just because something is written down or shared widely doesn't make it true. It's about taking that extra moment to think before reacting, something my years of experience have taught me is always worthwhile.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As someone who doesn't use social media myself, I've watched from the sidelines as my children and grandchildren navigate this tricky world of online information. From my chair at the salon, I hear clients talk about things they've seen online that just don't sit right with me. Managing fake content starts with good old-fashioned common sense, the kind my parents taught me. When I hear something that sounds too good to be true or makes me feel anxious, I pause and think it through. I remember my mother saying 'consider the source' - that applies just as much to online posts as it did to neighborhood gossip. Since I'm naturally curious but also get worried easily, I've learned to channel that curiosity into checking facts rather than letting anxiety take over. I ask my husband or one of my kids to help me look things up on their computers when I hear something questionable. At work, when clients share wild stories they've seen online, I gently suggest they check with reliable sources like our local newspaper or news stations. My Christian faith teaches me about truth and honesty, and I think those values guide how I approach information - if something feels dishonest or manipulative, it probably is. I've taught my children to be skeptical of anything that tries to play on their emotions too strongly, especially since I know how easily anxiety can cloud judgment. The board games we play as a family have taught us all to think several moves ahead, to consider consequences - that same thinking helps when evaluating online content. My painting hobby has shown me how perspective matters; what looks one way from one angle might be completely different from another. Really, dealing with fake content comes down to trusting your gut, asking questions, and remembering that just because something is written down or shared widely doesn't make it true. It's about taking that extra moment to think before reacting, something my years of experience have taught me is always worthwhile.
